results.title: Attacker can reenter to mint all the collection supply
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit’s Item 24 (“Per-Address Public Mint Limit Bypass via _safeMint Reentrancy Callback”) describes the same reentrancy due to CEI violation in NextGenCore::mint: the per-address (and allowlist) counters are incremented after calling _safeMint, enabling an ERC721Receiver reentry to MinterContract::mint and bypassing maxCollectionPurchases to mint arbitrarily many tokens up to collection supply. This matches the described attack path (MinterContract::mint -> NextGenCore::_safeMint -> onERC721Received -> reenter mint), root cause (state updated after external call), impact (mint entire supply), and fix (move effects before interaction/CEI). Fixing the audit-reported issue would fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: Attacker can drain all ETH from AuctionDemo when block.timestamp == auctionEndTime
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item #3 describes the same root cause and code path: at block.timestamp == auctionEndTime both claimAuction (>=) and cancelBid (<=) are callable, and claimAuction’s refund loop does not set bid status to false. This enables double refunds by combining claimAuction with cancelBid, allowing the attacker to drain ETH and get the NFT for free. It cites the exact functions (AuctionDemo.claimAuction and cancelBid), the timestamp overlap, and the missing status update. The proposed/implicit fixes (use strict inequality and set status=false before refunding, possibly with a reentrancy guard) would also fix the vulnerability described in H-02. Thus, the report detected the same issue.
